coredata.dc:title: A sensitive and low toxicity electrochemical sensor for 2,4-dichlorophenol based on the nanocomposite of carbon dots, hexadecyltrimethyl ammonium bromide and chitosan
coredata.prism:copyright: Copyright © 2015 Elsevier B.V. All rights reserved.
coredata.dc:creator: Yu, Lanlan
Yue, Xiu
Yang, Ran
Jing, Shasha
Qu, Lingbo
coredata.dc:description: A novel and sensitive electrochemical sensor for 2,4-dichlorophenol (2,4-DCP) based on a nanocomposite of carbon dots (CDs), hexadecyltrimethyl ammonium bromide (CTAB) and chitosan (CS) was constructed through electrostatic self-assembly technology. The nanocomposite of CDs–CTAB–CS introduced a favorable access for the electron transfer and exhibited excellent electrocatalytic activity toward the oxidation of 2,4-DCP. Differential pulse voltammetry (DPV) was used for the quantitative determination of 2,4-DCP. Under the optimized experimental conditions, the oxidation peak currents of 2,4-DCP were proportional to its concentrations in the range from 0.04 to 8.0μmolL−1 with a detection limit as 0.01μmolL−1. The newly developed method was successfully applied for the determination of 2,4-DCP in waste water with satisfactory recoveries. This CDs–CTAB–CS modified electrochemical sensor provides an operational access to design simpler, low toxicity and environment-friendly sensors based on carbon dots.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
2,4-Dichlorophenol
Electrochemical sensor
Environment-friendly sensor

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Influence of the ratio of CDs to CTAB and the amount of CDs–CTAB
Effect of scan rate and reaction mechanism of 2,4-DCP

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A) TEM image of CDs. (B) Particle size distribution of the prepared CDs by anoplus zeta/nano particle analyzer. (C) FT-IR spectrum of CDs. (D) UV–vis absorption spectra of CDs, CTAB and CTAB–CDs.

Nyquist plots of bare GCE, CTAB/GCE, CDs/GCE, CS/GCE, CDs–CTAB/GCE and CS/CDs–CTAB/GCE in 5.0mmolL−1 [Fe(CN)6]3−/4− solution containing 0.1molL−1 KCl. Frequency ranges from 0.01 to 105 Hz; initiative potential, 0.24V; amplitude, 0.01V and quiet time of 2s.

Cyclic voltammetric behaviors of 2,4-DCP (2.0μmolL−1) in 0.1molL−1 PBS (pH 7.0) on the bare GCE, CTAB/GCE, CDs/GCE, CS/GCE, CDs–CTAB/GCE and CS/CDs–CTAB/GCE. Accumulation time: 120s; scan rate: 100mVs−1.

(A) The effect of pH value on the oxidation current of 10μmolL−1 2,4-DCP in 0.1molL−1 PBS on the CS/CDs–CTAB/GCE. Inset: Peak current with respect to pH. (B) Peak potential with respect to pH. Scan rate: 100mVs−1.

(A) CVs of 10μmolL−1 2,4-DCP in 0.1molL−1 PBS (pH 7.0) on the CS/CDs–CTAB/GCE at various scan rates. Curve numbers (from a to g) represent: 20, 40, 60, 80,100, 150, and 200mVs−1, respectively. (B) The plot shows the linear relationship between I pa and v 1/2, the inset expresses the relationship of E pa with respect to ln v.

DPV responses of 2,4-DCP with increasing concentrations in 0.1molL−1 PBS (pH 7.0) on the CS/CDs–CTAB/GCE. The concentrations of 2,4-DCP (from a to m): 0.04, 0.06, 0.08, 0.1, 0.2, 0.4, 0.6, 0.8, 1, 2, 4, 6 and 8μmolL−1, respectively. Inset: the calibration curve of the DPV responses of 2,4-DCP.

(A) Column chart of the oxidation peak current of 2.0μmolL−1 2,4-DCP in 0.1molL−1 PBS solution (pH 7.0), the PBS solution containing 1.0mmolL−1 metal ions. (B) The electrochemical behaviors of common phenols (2.0μmolL−1) at the CS/CDs–CTAB/GCE in the 0.1molL−1 PBS (pH 7.0).
Schematic illustration of CDs–CTAB–CS composites and the oxidation process for 2,4-DCP on CS/CDs–CTAB/GCE.
Performance comparison of the fabricated electrode for 2,4-DCP detection with the other reported electrodes.

ce:legend.ce:simple-para: MB-AG, myoglobin and agarose; HRP, horseradish peroxidase; MWNTs, multiwalled carbon nanotubues; Lac, laccase; PVA, polyvinyl alcohol; F108, polyethylene oxide–polyoxypropylene–polyethylene oxide (PEO–PPO–PEO); Au NPs, gold nanoparticles; MIP, molecularly imprinted polymers.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Recovery of 2,4-DCP in waste water samples (n =5).

head.ce:title: A sensitive and low toxicity electrochemical sensor for 2,4-dichlorophenol based on the nanocomposite of carbon dots, hexadecyltrimethyl ammonium bromide and chitosan

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Chemistry and Molecular Engineering, Zhengzhou University, Zhengzhou 450001, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Chemistry and Molecular Engineering, Zhengzhou University

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author at: College of Chemistry and Molecular Engineering, Zhengzhou University, Kexue Road No. 100, Zhengzhou 450001, PR China.

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Chemistry and Molecular Engineering, Zhengzhou University

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A novel and sensitive electrochemical sensor for 2,4-dichlorophenol (2,4-DCP) based on a nanocomposite of carbon dots (CDs), hexadecyltrimethyl ammonium bromide (CTAB) and chitosan (CS) was constructed through electrostatic self-assembly technology. The nanocomposite of CDs–CTAB–CS introduced a favorable access for the electron transfer and exhibited excellent electrocatalytic activity toward the oxidation of 2,4-DCP. Differential pulse voltammetry (DPV) was used for the quantitative determination of 2,4-DCP. Under the optimized experimental conditions, the oxidation peak currents of 2,4-DCP were proportional to its concentrations in the range from 0.04 to 8.0μmolL−1 with a detection limit as 0.01μmolL−1. The newly developed method was successfully applied for the determination of 2,4-DCP in waste water with satisfactory recoveries. This CDs–CTAB–CS modified electrochemical sensor provides an operational access to design simpler, low toxicity and environment-friendly sensors based on carbon dots.

ce:section.ce:para: 2,4-Dichlorophenol (2,4-DCP) is a typical chlorinated phenol that is extensively used in many industries, such as pharmaceuticals, pesticides, fungicides and insecticides [1]. It has been listed as prior pollutants by the US Environment Protection Agency (EPA) due to its properties such as highly toxic, poorly biodegradable and potentially carcinogenic [2]. The maximum admissible concentration of 2,4-DCP in drinking water is regulated at 0.5ngmL−1 [3]. 2,4-DCP is easily absorbed by skin, poses a serious ecological problem as an environmental pollutant, and can seriously affect both public health and environmental quality. Many analytical methods have been established to determine 2,4-DCP, such as gas chromatography (GC) [4], UV spectrophotometry [5], high performance liquid chromatography (HPLC) [6], capillary electrophoresis (CE) [7] and electrochemical methods [1,8–15]. Most of these methods are low sensitive and of complex manipulation, due to that 2,4-DCP is usually present in low concentrations or mixed with lots of interfering substances. Electrochemical methods may be more preferable, due to its fast response, simple operation and low cost [1]. However, the electrode modified materials are usually complex, difficult to immobilize on electrode surfaces, or may be easily denatured when experimental conditions are not carefully controlled. All these factors show the essential to investigate novel electrode materials for the determination of 2,4-DCP in environmental samples.

As a novel fluorescent nanoparticles, carbon dots (CDs) were accidently discovered during the electrophoretic purification of single-wall carbon nanotubes derived from arc-discharge soot [16]. In recent years, researches about synthetic pathways of CDs have been proposed constantly to find more inexpensive and simpler synthetic routes [17,18]. And as a newly emerged fluorescent nanoparticles, CDs have shown enormous potential for a wide range of applications, including bioimaging [19–21], biosensing [22], chemical sensing [23–32], photocatalysis and electrocatalysis [33]. Properties of CDs, such as low toxicity, environmental friendliness, chemical stability, make it possible to develop highly sensitive sensing platforms based on it [34]. And due to its low cytotoxicity, excellent biocompatibility, simple synthesis, economy and remarkable conductivity, CDs have been used for rapid, simple and sensitive determination of DA [35]. Carbon dots, together with graphene and carbon nanotubes, are one of carbon nanomaterials, which might display comparable electrocatalytic activity as graphene and carbon nanotubes. However, the exploration of CDs as electrochemical sensors still remains at an early stage [35–37].

Hexadecyltrimethyl ammonium bromide (CTAB) was used as an absorbent for phenols due to its strong hydrophobic interaction between the long alkyl chain of CTAB and aromatic ring of phenols [38,40]. As a cationic surfactant, CTAB can be used as electrode modifier for enrichment of phenols on the electrode surface. Li has developed a sensitive electrochemical sensor for the determination of chlorophenols with CTAB as the electrode material [40]. In this work, we integrated CDs and CTAB for sensor fabrication through the electrostatic self-assembly between them, considering the positive charge of CTAB could make it easily assembled with electronegative CDs. Due to the good water-solubility of both CDs and CTAB, which makes this composite material easily peel off from electrode surface, chitosan (CS) was applied as fixative and combined with CDs and CTAB to fabricate modified electrode. The CDs–CTAB–CS electrode gave great electrocatalytic activity toward the oxidation of 2,4-DCP, representing a new electrochemical platform for designing low toxicity and environment-friendly electrochemical sensors.

2,4-Dichlorophenol (2,4-DCP), 2-chlorophenol (2-CP), pentachlorophenol (PCP), bisphenol A, pyrocatechol, hydroquinol, and phloroglucinol were obtained from Sigma–Aldrich. Sucrose, oleic, hexane, hexadecyltrimethyl ammonium bromide (CTAB), and chitosan (CS) were obtained from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Beijing Co., Ltd. The dialysis membrane (Da=1000) was purchased from Beijing Solarbio Science & Technology Co., Ltd. All the other chemicals and reagents used in the experiments were of analytical grade and were used as received. Double distilled water was used throughout this study.

A Shimadzu UV-2550s Spectrophotometer was used for acquiring the UV–vis absorption spectra. The FT-IR spectrum was acquired on a FT-IR-8400 Shimadzu Fourier transform spectroscopy. A Nanoplus zeta/nano particle analyzer (USA) was used for size analysis. Transmission electron microscope (TEM) images were acquired on JEM-2100 (Japan) with a 200kV accelerating voltage.

All of the voltammetric measurements were implemented on an RST 3000 electrochemical system (Suzhou Risetech Instrument Co., Ltd., China). A CHI660E electrochemical workstation (Shanghai Chenhua Co., China) was used for the AC impedance spectroscopy. A three-electrode configuration was employed, including a bare glassy carbon electrode (GCE) (diameter of 4mm) or a CS/CDs–CTAB/GCE as the working electrode, a saturated calomel electrode (SCE) as the reference electrode and a platinum wire electrode as the auxiliary electrode. All of the pH values were measured with a PHS-3C precision pH meter (Leici Devices Factory of Shanghai, China).

Carbon dots (CDs) were synthesized according to Chen's method [34]. 20g sucrose and 40mL oil acid were put into a three neck flask, heating at 215°C for 5min until the sucrose slowly melted and turned from an orange suspension to a clear brownish solution. Supernatant liquid was discharged and the brownish precipitate was obtained at the bottom of flask. 40mL water was added to dissolve the precipitate and hexane was added to extract the dispersion for several times in order to remove residual oil acid. Then the brown dispersion was dialyzed against pure water through a dialysis membrane (Da=1000) for 24h to remove nonreacted small particles. Finally, the resulted dispersion was freeze-dried in vacuum for 48h and the final product was obtained as a brown powder.
CDs–CTAB composite was obtained by adding 0.67mL CTAB (1.0mmolL−1) into 1mL of the CDs solution (10mgmL−1) and sonicating for 1h to yield a uniform suspension.

Before modification, the bare GCE was polished to a mirror-like appearance with a 0.05μm alumina slurry, then washed successively with anhydrous alcohol and redistilled water in an ultrasonic bath for 1min, then dried. The working electrode was prepared as follows: with a microinjector, 10μL of CDs–CTAB mixture was added onto the prepared GCE surface and dried. Then, 10μL of 1.0% CS solutions were coated onto the CDs–CTAB/GCE and dried under infrared lamp. The obtained electrode is referred to as CS/CDs–CTAB/GCE. The other modified electrode followed the similar procedure except adding different modified materials.

An appropriate volume of 2,4-DCP solution was diluted by PBS (0.1molL−1, pH 7.0) to prepare a 2,4-DCP solution with desired concentration. About 5mL of the solution was added into the electrochemical cell, and then the three-electrode system was installed in it. Lake water sample was obtained from Mei Lake in Zhengzhou. It was pretreated by filtration and centrifugation, and then the pH was adjusted to 7 by PBS. At last, a certain concentration of EDTA was added into above solution to eliminate the interference of some possible inorganic ions.

Cyclic voltammetry (CV) was employed between 0.2 and 1.2V with a scan rate of 0.1Vs−1. Differential pulse voltammetry (DPV) measurements were performed from 0.2 to 1.2V with these following parameters: increment potential, 4mV; pulse amplitude, 0.05V; pulse width, 0.04V; pulse period, 0.1s; sample width, 10ms; quiet time, 3s. AC impedance was performed in a 5.0mmolL−1 Fe(CN)6 3−/4− (1:1) solution containing 0.1molL−1 KCl with these parameters: frequency range from 0.01 to 105 Hz; initiative potential, 0.24V; amplitude, 0.01V and quiet time of 2s.

The morphology of the CDs was characterized by TEM (Fig. 1A). The image of CDs demonstrated well dispersed and spherical nanoparticles in narrow distributions of 1.55±0.25nm, as shown in Fig. 1B. The FT-IR spectrum of CDs was given in Fig. 1C to identify the functional groups of CDs. Peaks at 3418cm−1 and 1648cm−1 are attributed to the stretching vibrations of OH and CO, indicating the existence of large numbers of hydroxyl groups on CDs surface, which stabilized the CDs in aqueous solution. Peaks at 1411cm−1 and 1038cm−1 correspond to the asymmetric and symmetric stretching vibrations of COC, respectively [34]. These fundamental absorptions suggest the successful synthesis of CDs. UV–visible absorption spectroscopy was performed for further investigations of CDs. As shown in Fig. 1D, CDs displayed a characteristic absorption band at 225nm, corresponding to the formation of multiple polyaromatic chromophores. The peak at 280nm may be attributed to the π–π* transition of CDs [34]. All these results indicated that CDs have been successfully synthesized.

AC impedance is an efficient tool to study the interface properties of surface-modified electrodes. The charge-transfer resistance (R ct) at the electrode surface can be used to describe the interface properties of the electrode [39]. After being fitted with an equivalent electrical circuit, the R ct for all the electrodes were 46.91Ω for the bare GCE, 40.29Ω for the CTAB/GCE, 22.27Ω for the CS/GCE, 1191Ω for the CDs/GCE, 227.1Ω for the CDs–CTAB/GCE, 202.5Ω for the CS/CDs–CTAB/GCE. The obvious difference of the semicircle portion at higher frequencies among CDs/GCE, CDs–CTAB/GCE and CS/CDs–CTAB/GCE can be seen in Fig. 2 , demonstrating that the modification of CDs–CTAB–CS on the surface of GCE has been successfully completed.

The almost linear Nyquist plot of CS/GCE indicates that the chitosan can greatly improve the electron transfer rate. This improvement was probably due to the positive charge of CS, which promotes the access of [Fe(CN)6]3−/4− to the electrode surface. For the CDs/GCE, the semicircle portion at higher frequencies remarkably increased. This increase was probably due to the negative charge of CDs, which prevents the access of [Fe(CN)6]3−/4− to the electrode surface [34]. For CDs–CTAB/GCE, the semicircle portion at higher frequencies remarkably decreased, which suggests that the CDs–CTAB composite introduced a favorable access for electron transfer [40].

The electrochemical behavior of 2,4-DCP at the GCE, CS/GCE, CTAB/GCE, CDs/GCE, CDs–CTAB/GCE and CS/CDs–CTAB/GCE in 0.1molL−1 PBS (pH 7.0) were studied using CV. The cyclic voltammograms are depicted in Fig. 3 . As illustrated in Fig. 3, oxidation peak of 2,4-DCP on bare GCE was very weak at 0.71V. It was found that when CDs or CTAB were applied individually, the oxidation peak current of 2,4-DCP increased for either modification, exhibiting good electrocatalytic activity of CDs and CTAB toward 2,4-DCP. However, when CDs–CTAB nanocomposite was modified on the electrode, the oxidation current of 2,4-DCP on CDs–CTAB/GCE was dramatically enhanced, much higher than that of the sum of CDs and CTAB, which indicates that the CDs–CTAB nanocomposite was synergistic for electron transfer.

Further investigation showed that the stability of CDs–CTAB/GCE was poor and the oxidation current gradually decreased in successive measurements. This phenomenon was the result from the loss of the CDs–CTAB composite from the electrode surface, leading to easy dissolution of the CDs–CTAB composite into PBS solution. A frequently used electrode fixative, chitosan was cast onto the surface of CDs–CTAB/GCE to protect against the loss of the CDs–CTAB composite. The CS/CDs–CTAB/GCE exhibited improved and good stability. The CS acted as an electrode fixative for this sensor, and it was found that after modification with CS, the oxidation current of 2,4-DCP increased.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Influence of the ratio of CDs to CTAB and the amount of CDs–CTAB
ce:section.ce:para: Different CDs–CTAB were prepared by adding different amounts of CTAB (1.0mmolL−1) into 1mL of the CDs solution (10mgmL−1). 10μL of the CDs–CTAB solution was used for the fabrication of CS/CDs–CTAB/GCE. By comparing the electrochemical response of 2,4-DCP obtained on CS/CDs–CTAB/GCE, the optimum CDs to CTAB ratio was determined. When the ratio of CDs to CTAB was 3:2, the oxidation current of 2,4-DCP was maximized, so this ratio of CDs to CTAB for preparing the CDs–CTAB composites was selected.

The amount of CDs–CTAB composite solution on GCE surface ranged from 5.0 to 12.0μL was also studied and the oxidation current of 2,4-DCP reached maximum at 10.0μL. So, 10.0μL CDs–CTAB (3:2) composite solution was used for further studies.

Influence of different fixatives including PVP, Nafion, CS and β-cyclodextrin was investigated to obtain the optimum electrochemical response. All of them produced inhibitive effect to some extent on the oxidation of 2,4-DCP except chitosan. The chitosan provided good fixation for the CDs–CTAB composite on the electrode surface, maintaining good response and stability. Therefore, chitosan was used as the best fixative in the present study.

In order to increase the sensitivity of determination, the effect of supporting electrolyte phosphate buffer solution (PBS) (0.1molL−1, pH range from 3.0 to 9.0) was investigated using CV (Fig. 4A). For the PBS solution in the pH range of 3.0–9.0, the oxidation peak current reached the maximum value at pH 7.0, while the oxidation peak potential shifted negatively with the increased pH.

The regression equation of the peak potential (E pa) and pH can be expressed as: E pa (V)=−0.0531V/pH+1.1347V (R =0.9989) (Fig. 4B). The value of the slope (−53.1mV/pH) was close to the theoretical Nerstian value of −59mV/pH, which indicated that the total numbers of electrons and protons taking part in the oxidation mechanism were the same [8].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effect of scan rate and reaction mechanism of 2,4-DCP
ce:section.ce:para: The influence of scan rate on oxidation of 2,4-DCP was studied by CV to investigate the reaction kinetics and reaction mechanism. As shown in Fig. 5A, the anodic peak intensity increased continuously with increased scan rate. The regression equation is: I pa (μA)=0.5895v 1/2 (mVs−1)−0.5265 (R =0.9958). A good linear relationship between the peak current and the square root of the scan rate (v 1/2) is achieved in Fig. 5B, suggesting the oxidation process of 2,4-DCP at the CS/CDs–CTAB/GCE is a typical diffusion controlled process [11].

Moreover, the effect of scan rate on the oxidation peak potential was also investigated (Fig. 5B inset). With the increase of scan rate, the oxidation peak potential shifted positively, and the E pa and ln v exhibited a linear relationship. The linear regression equation is: E pa (V)=0.0245ln v +0.6510 (R =0.9901). According to the Laviron's theory, the E pa of the totally irreversible electrode process could be described by the Laviron equation:
E
pa
=
E 0
−
R T
( 1 − α ) n F
ln
R T
k s
( 1 − α ) n F
+
R T
( 1 − α ) n F
ln v
For which n is the electron transfer number, α is the electron transfer coefficient, k s is the standard rate coefficient. Firstly (1− α)n was calculated to be 1.013 according to the slope of the plot of E pa with respect to ln v. Generally, for a totally irreversible electrode process, α is assumed to be 0.5 [42]. Then the electron transfer number n is obtained to be 2. Therefore, similar to the previous report, the oxidation process of 2,4-DCP on CS/CDs–CTAB/GCE is a two-electron and two-proton process [41]. The mechanism of the reaction on this electrode is deduced and depicted in Scheme 1 .
According to Fig. 6 , DPV was utilized for the determination of 2,4-DCP with the CS/CDs–CTAB/GCE, due to its higher current sensitivity and better resolution than CV.

Under the optimum conditions, the linearity between the peak current of 2,4-DCP and its concentration can be expressed as: I pa (μA)=0.6026c (μmolL−1)+0.4869 (R =0.9947) (Fig. 6 inset). The linear relationship between peak currents (I pa) and 2,4-DCP concentration was in the range from 0.04 to 8.0μmolL−1 and the limit of detection was estimated to be 0.01μmolL−1 (S/N=3) [43].

Compared with other electrochemical methods (Table 1 ), the proposed CS/CDs–CTAB/GCE showed higher sensitivity, wider linear range and a simpler electrode fabrication process. It is not the most sensitive electrochemical system, but it has higher environmental friendliness and lower toxicity. Furthermore, the carbon dots have low toxicity and simpler synthetic procedure, which makes it possible to represent a new platform for designing environment-friendly electrochemical sensors.

Potential interferences of some possible inorganic ions and organic compounds in real samples were investigated by analyzing a standard solution of 2.0μmolL−1 2,4-DCP in 0.1molL−1 PBS solution (pH 7.0) to assess the possible analytical application of the proposed sensor (Fig. 7 ).

Metal ions have negligible effects on the peak current of 2,4-DCP in large concentration (1.0mmolL−1) except for Cu2+ (Fig. 7 A). EDTA was added into the detection system to eliminate the interference of Cu2+. It was found that the current of 2,4-DCP remained unchanged when 1.0mmolL−1 Cu2+ was present after the addition of 5.0mmolL−1 EDTA. So 5.0mmolL−1 EDTA was added into the supporting electrolyte of 0.1molL−1 PBS (pH 7.0) in these and next experiments.

Considering that the structurally similar phenols maybe have similar electrochemical responses and result in the interference with the determination of 2,4-DCP, electrochemical behaviors of common phenols such as 2-CP, pentachlorophenol (PCP), bisphenol A, pyrocatechol, hydroquinol, and phloroglucinol were also investigated at the same determination conditions.

As shown in Fig. 7B, pyrocatechol, hydroquinol, and phloroglucinol have no obvious DPV electrochemical response between 0.2 and 1.2V. PCP has no influence on the determination of the 2,4-DCP due to its oxidation potential far enough from that of the 2,4-DCP. For bisphenol A and 2-CP, with the similar oxidation potentials to that of 2,4-DCP, its electrochemical signal was smaller than that of 2,4-DCP, which indeed caused some interference with the detection of 2,4-DCP. Therefore, to some extent, the CS/CDs–CTAB/GCE may be used for selective determination of 2,4-DCP, but the selectivity of this modified electrode should be further improved. The proposed sensor is applicable in the determination of 2,4-DCP in the absence of bisphenol A and 2-CP.

To investigate the applicability of the proposed method for the practical determination of 2,4-DCP, the CS/CDs–CTAB/GCE established in this study was applied to detect 2.4-DCP in water samples. After PBS solution was added into lake water sample to adjust pH, 5mmolL−1 EDTA was added to eliminate the interference of metal ions. It was found that no obvious DPV electrochemical response was obtained in the water sample.

The recovery experiments were performed to evaluate the proposed method by adding known concentrations of 2,4-DCP into the pretreated lake water sample. The recoveries for 2,4-DCP by DPV method ranged from 96.90% to 106.60% (Table 2 ), suggesting the novel modified electrode of CS/CDs–CTAB/GCE possesses excellent reliability and promising applicability.

A novel, sensitive and selective electrochemical sensor for 2,4-DCP was developed based on the nanocomposite of CDs and CTAB through electrostatic self-assembly and fixed by chitosan. In contrast to previous reports, the proposed electrochemical sensor showed much improved catalytic oxidation toward 2,4-DCP, and it was more sensitive, simpler, and environment-friendly. This CDs–CTAB–CS electrode system explored the great potential applications of CDs for electrochemical sensors, representing a new platform for designing low toxicity and environment-friendly electrochemical sensors.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors would like to thank the financial supports from the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 21002093 and No. U1404214).

Preparation and characterization of the dopamine film electrochemically deposited on a gold template and its applications for dopamine sensing in aqueous solution
Electrochemical oxidation behavior of bisphenol A at surfactant/layered double hydroxide modified glassy carbon electrode and its determination

A novel and sensitive hexadecyltrimethyl ammonium bromide functionalized graphene supported platinum nanoparticles composite modified glassy carbon electrode for determination of sunset yellow in soft drinks

ce:biography.ce:simple-para: Lanlan Yu is an associate professor at College of Chemistry and Molecular Engineering of Zhengzhou University, China. Her current research interests focus on nano-electrochemistry and biochemistry.
Xiu Yue is currently working toward the master degree at College of Chemistry and Molecular Engineering of Zhengzhou University, China. Her research interests focus on electrochemical sensor.
Ran Yang is an associate professor at College of Chemistry and Molecular Engineering of Zhengzhou University, China. Her current research interests focus on nano-electrochemistry.
Shasha Jing is currently working toward the master degree at College of Chemistry and Molecular Engineering of Zhengzhou University, China. Her research interests focus on electrochemical sensor.
Lingbo Qu is a professor at College of Chemistry and Molecular Engineering of Zhengzhou University, China. His current research interests focus on medicinal chemistry.
